claims.claim_text: The opening outage anecdote presents a concrete reboot incident with a specific time and operational state, but no attributable source.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: At 03:10, an SRE is paged because a Kubernetes node has stopped admitting pods after a routine reboot. The kubelet process is running, the container runtime starts, and the application team insists nothing changed in their manifests.
claims.suggested_rewrite: An SRE may be paged after a routine reboot when a Kubernetes node stops admitting pods even though kubelet and the container runtime still appear to be running normally.
claims.rationale: This reads like a real incident with precise situational detail but no attributable source, so the false specificity should be removed.

claims.claim_text: The same anecdote adds an unattributed payments-processor scenario with a specific settlement-window impact.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The company is a regional payments processor, so every minute of degraded node capacity pushes more traffic onto a smaller pool and increases payment latency during the morning settlement window.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In a revenue-critical environment, degraded node capacity can push more traffic onto fewer healthy nodes and increase user-facing latency.
claims.rationale: The named business context and concrete operational consequence are illustrative but unattributed, so the specifics should be removed.

claims.claim_text: On x86 systems, privileged kernel code runs at ring 0 while ordinary user processes run at ring 3.
claims.span_hint: section: Kernel Space, User Space, and System Calls
claims.rationale: The cited page directly describes ring-based privilege levels and the ring 0/ring 3 distinction used on x86.

claims.claim_text: The initramfs stage provides early userspace that helps locate and mount the real root filesystem.
claims.span_hint: section: Boot: From Firmware to a Usable System
claims.rationale: The Linux kernel documentation directly supports this boot-stage behavior, but the primary source host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Linux kernel is monolithic in architecture while also being modular through loadable kernel modules.
claims.span_hint: section: Architecture Choices: Monolithic, Modular, and Microkernel Designs
claims.rationale: The cited page explicitly notes that the Linux kernel is both monolithic and modular.

claims.claim_text: kube-proxy requires IPVS to be available on the node before it can run in IPVS mode.
claims.span_hint: section: Kubernetes-Relevant Kernel Capabilities, Worked Diagnosis: kube-proxy Cannot Use IPVS
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes networking reference states that IPVS must be available on the node before starting kube-proxy in IPVS mode.

claims.claim_text: cgroup v2 is the recommended modern cgroup mode for Kubernetes, and some Kubernetes features rely on cgroup v2 primitives.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes cgroup v2 page says cgroup v2 is recommended and that some features exclusively use cgroup v2.

claims.claim_text: Linux containers share the machine's OS kernel rather than packaging a separate kernel per application.
claims.span_hint: section: Containers Share the Host Kernel
claims.rationale: Docker's container overview explicitly states that containers share the machine's OS kernel.

claims.claim_text: Privileged containers override or ignore seccomp, AppArmor, and SELinux protections on Linux nodes.
claims.span_hint: section: Containers Share the Host Kernel
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes security page directly lists how privileged containers override seccomp, AppArmor, and SELinux constraints.
The cited version-history page directly gives Linux 1.0.0's release date as March 1994.

claims.claim_text: /proc is a special virtual filesystem that exposes live process and kernel state rather than ordinary stored files.
claims.rationale: The procfs page directly describes /proc as a special filesystem that presents dynamic process and kernel information.

claims.claim_text: PID 1 is special because the kernel directly executes the initial userspace init process during boot.
claims.rationale: The Linux kernel documentation directly describes executing init from early rootfs as PID 1, but the primary source host is off the current allowlist.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes: About cgroup v2
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the clearest Kubernetes reference for modern Linux resource-control expectations on nodes.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes: Linux kernel security constraints for Pods and containers
further_reading.why_relevant: It connects shared-kernel container security to seccomp, AppArmor, SELinux, and privileged containers.

further_reading.title: Docker: What is a Container?
further_reading.why_relevant: It is a concise allowlisted explanation of the shared-kernel model that underpins the container sections in this module.

notes: No shared citation pool reference was provided, so claims carry direct proposed URLs. For kernel-boot specifics, the cleanest primary sources are kernel.org docs; those two claims are flagged as allowlist-expansion candidates.
